sentences.text: Ralph, Rafael--
My name is Ralph, Rafael, Lewin, L-E-W-I-N.
I was born in Moscow on February the 16th, 1925, in Moscow.
When I was approximately four years old my father had to run away from Russia because he was a capitalist.
And he had a factory manufacturing textiles.
And somehow because of this he had contacts with somebody in Riga.
Riga, that is in Lithuania.
And he went down there in 1925-- '28, 1928.
And just a little bit later we got the message from him that we should leave Moscow with the train.
And we went over the border to Riga.
I am now 72 years old.
Should I go on?
And from Riga we went to my aunt in Germany, to Heidelberg.
My father, my mother, and I.
My father's name is [?
Maksim ?] Lewin.
My mother's name is Anna Lewin.
So when we came to Heidelberg it was 1929, to my aunt.
Her name was Anna Sulkes, S-U-L-K-E-S.
And approximately three, four months later, we moved to Mannheim.
Mannheim is in Germany.
Mannheim, Germany, in Baden.
And in 1931 I went to school.
That was my first year.
It was just a public school.
At that time we lived by a friend of my father's.
And in Mannheim, the streets are actually blocks.
And the block we lived in at that time was Block S.
And because it was on the main street, it was called S1.
And the house number was 8.
And we stayed there for approximately a year.
At that time I started to go to school.
And then I went to school.
It was called the U Block.
U2 school.
And that was a public school.
So I went there just for one year.
And we moved from this place.
And we moved to K, to the block K.
And I lived in K4 because it was the fourth block from the main street, K4.
And the number was 8.
And I did not change schools.
So I went to the U school.
And the people-- the boys and the girls in this district, they went to the K school.
So when I was approximately seven years old, that was when I was in grade 2, I made friends with the boys from that street.
And we went many times to the river Neckar, which was approximately two blocks away from my home.
And we played.
And so was not too bad at that time.
That was in 1932.
And Hitler was not in power yet.
And nobody was calling me names.
And I learned German pretty quick.
And I became one of the boys.
In school, we had in my class at that time three Jews.
And naturally, we kept most of the time to ourselves.
But we also had Christian friends.
And it went very good.
And we became quite friendly, all the class.
And a year later, in 1934, when Hitler was already in power, it was not too bad either.
We were good friends.
And naturally, we had a few guys which were anti-Semitic, and they called me names.
[GERMAN] and [GERMAN], and so on.
But there were the other boys, I always said to them, look, don't start.
We are pretty good friends here, and we help each other in school and in the lessons, and leave that out, you see.
And the boys listened.
So now it started to become a little bit worse because the propaganda in Germany was definitely against Jews.
So some of these children, they brought a newspaper to school which was called And at that time, they were reading some articles.
And so the boys became a little bit uneasy to defend a Jew.
But no fights ever came out of that, except we were a little bit more separated.
But I had one of these boys, a Christian boy, he was on the top of the school.
He was just with me all the time-- in math and science and most other subjects.
And we were very, very, very good friends.
And we also went to see the family, his family.
And I did not want him to come to my family, because, as you understand, we were refugees from Russia.
We didn't have much.
My father lost everything in Russia and had to leave everything.
So I was a little bit ashamed to bring him to my place where we lived.
But he understood.
He always called me to his place.
And he had a sister.
She was about two years older than him.
And she was very, very nice.
And we were so used to it, we asked all kinds of questions, and so on.
She always explained to us what is the right thing to do, and so on.
And that went on for quite a while.
And finally, my father, somehow, became a friend of his father because my father was giving lectures in a university in Mannheim in Russian and Latin and Greek, and also English and French.
And so my father, he always bought a French newspaper.
And that was the only paper what was allowed into Germany after 1933 when Hitler took to power.
This paper was called [FRENCH].
And just the name of the paper [FRENCH].
So I always went to get this paper.
And when my father finished reading this paper, I brought it always over to my friend's house, and his father was always reading the paper.
And sometimes they were talking about things, what was going on.
And that was very nice, till 1936. 1936, after the Nuremberg loss, my--
My life?
Till then, not very much.
I was still friends with the boys in the K district.
And I didn't have too many friends in the U district.
He lived a little bit in the better district from the U district, for the schools in the U district.
I didn't have too many friends in that district over there, except him.
So when I went to visit him, I always went by bike to visit him, you see.
And when we were playing on the street or by the river, I was with the friends from this district.
And somehow these people from this district, they didn't make no difference.
How did your life change after the Nuremberg-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] Ah-ha.
Now, this is the question.
Why would the friends what knew me so good, after the Nuremberg loss, change?
They changed.
Some of them changed a lot.
They started to call me 'Jew.' [GERMAN] and all kinds of names.
But I have to say that nobody actually attacked me.
Nobody actually beated me up.
And my friend was still my friend.
But when I went to visit them, I noticed that his sister, she had to join the BDM.
That's the Bund Deutscher Madel.
That means the Club of German Girls.
After she had to join, because otherwise-- the father also had to join the party, the NSDAP-- the Hitler party.
Yeah.
When-- my friend had to go also to the Hitler Youth, so they all were in the Party-- his father, the sister, and him.
Now, the father did not change.
My friend did not change.
But his sister, she changed.
She said to me like this, you know, you're a Jew.
And I don't want you to play with my brother.
And I don't want you to come anymore to our house.
Well, I felt pretty bad.
But she somehow was the authority in the house.
She was the helper to her mother.
She was the baker, and the cook, and the bed-maker, and then, you know.
And so they all listened, you know.
But we made a pact with my friend.
And we said, well, from now on, when we meet we have to go in the forest, which was very close to us in Mannheim.
And we had a little place.
It was between bushes.
And nobody could see us.
It was a wild [INAUDIBLE].
And we met there.
And we had a couple of stones.
And we made a table and chairs to sit down, so that's where we met.
Now, the reason we met there is because we consulted each other about math, about science.
We talked about everything.
We talked about religion.
He brought with him, and I brought the French paper with me.
So I gave him the French paper, in secret.
He gave it to his father so the sister should not see it.
And he brought the and we were checking out the Now, I have to mention one very particular time he came.
And he said that, look what I brought you!
Look at what they say, that the Jews are making matzo, and they have to have from a Jewish girl's blood.
Otherwise, the matzo is not--
Yeah.
The matzo is not kosher.
So I said to him, look, I'm going to bring you the Bible.
And we're going to check.
I will show you that that is impossible.
And the next time we met-- we met, I would say, two times a week if the weather was nice.
And so I took my Bible, which was in Hebrew and in German, with explanations from the Talmud.
And I brought that with me.
And I showed it to him.
And I showed him that article that Jews, you should not eat blood.
So otherwise, the meat is not kosher, if the blood is not drained.
And I showed it to him.
And he said, I figured so.
I figured so.
But he said, what can I do?
I can't contradict because they're going to put me in jail.
I said, do nothing.
So fine.
Now, that was already in grade 5.
That was 1935.
Then it become worse in the school.

Now, we had one teacher, and he taught us social studies, and especially, German social studies, German history, and German history from-- from old, old times when-- when they had [GERMAN] stories, like Thor was the god from this time.
And one day, this teacher-- that was just before-- no.
That was-- yeah.
That was in 1935.
I believe that must've been just right after the Nuremberg loss.
That must have been just before Christmas, in November or in early December.
He came one time, and he said, to day we have to study the difference between us Christians, Aryan, between us Aryans and Jews.
That's right.
All three of us.
And he called out our names, and he called us to the front.
And he said, so now we're going to measure the nose.
We're going to measure the eyes.
We're going to measure the ears, the head.
And so they were discussing.
Well, just hold on one minute.
I will tell you how I felt.
So after he was almost finished with all these measurements and some of these children-- boys.
Only boys.
At that time, I have to mention, the schools were separated, the sexes, the only boys' school and girls' school.
So all of the boys, they came.
And they have to check themselves and look at the nose.
Then he said, now we will show you the most important part to find out if that is a Jew or not.
And then he ordered us to drop the pants.
Well, as I said before, I was born in Moscow.
And I was not circumcised because it was not allowed to have this religion in Russia at that time.
So when we dropped our pants, he-- the whole class started to laugh, because, you know, boys in Germany-- I don't know if it is in the other part.
But if we make sport, like football, or we play, we all have to go to make pee together.
OK?
So all the boys, they were taking out the penis and made pee.
So we knew the class, you know, that two of the Jewish boys, they were circumcised.
And they knew that I was not circumcised.
So as soon as we dropped the pants, they all laughed.
And the teacher was so mad, you know.
And he yelled at me, how come I'm not circumcised.
So I started to shake and to cry.
I said, because I was born in Moscow and in Moscow we was not allowed to-- to do religious rituals.
And the way he acted, I was so scared that he would take a knife and perform that operation right there on the spot.
And I looked at the boys, and they were laughing, and I was crying.
Definitely just that one boy, my friend.
He just hold his eyes and his head, and he just looked at me and he shook his head.
But why the rest of them were like this?
I knew, because he told me, when there were going to their meetings in the Hitler Youth, they taught them all kind of things.
Did you go-- Did you tell your parents about it when you went home?
No.
I was too ashamed.
What kind of what?
No-- yeah.
We had the holidays, but we were not very religious.
We had traditional.
We were traditional, and for all the holidays.
And my father explained me.
And as I just mentioned before, I had the Bible in the house.
And I studied the Bible a lot.
I knew I knew the Bible from A to Z.
And I studied all the comments from the Talmud and from different rabbis.
Oh, yes.
I knew my Bible very well.
We spoke three different languages.
My father spoke Jewish to me.
My mother spoke Russian to me.
And I answered in German, and sometimes in Jewish, and sometimes a little bit Russian.
[LAUGHS]
Oh, yes.
Yes.
But in German.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I was reading-- yeah.
I always liked to read.
I went to the library and got books, and so on, and also the new-- excuse me-- daily newspapers.
And I was very interested in what is happening around me and around in the world.
So now, that was one story in the school.
Now I will tell you a little story what happened to me with the boys from the K blocks.
Now, they had a friend.
Actually, it was a cousin, which had they-- they had-- his family-- had a garden outside of Mannheim.
Oh, that plot must have been about, I would say, five kilometers.
And in this garden, they were growing vegetables and flowers.
And it was not actually a very big garden.
It was a lot of little gardens.
The plot, I would say, must have been about maybe one kilometer long and one kilometer wide.
Inside it must have been about 100 little plots, where each one had a little fence.
And so they had there little garden, and so on.
So many times, I went with these people, with these kids, I went to that garden.
And in 1935, 1936, after the story with the Nuremberg loss, the boys what lived beside our house.
It was not a house.
It was another apartment block.
It was five stories high.
So they lived beside another apartment and another apartment.
They said to me, Ralph-- they called me Rafael.
Rafael, you know, this guy, the-- who lives next to the corner by the-- by the milk shop, he is one of the Hitler Youth leader.
Be very careful with him.
Never say anything that you are Jewish or anything.
So I said to them, I said, well, if we go and make pee, I don't have to worry.
He can't know that.
And so they said, oh, yeah.
OK.
Fine.
Good.
So we went to this garden.
And on the outside of the garden was little hills with grass and a little-- not actually a river, a little stream was going along.
And it they divided it to go into the gardens so that they can water the gardens.
And we had little snakes, garter snakes.
And we had also frogs.
And we had frogs with-- they have long tail with-- what is that called?
They look like little crocodiles.
[INAUDIBLE]
And so, anyhow--
